questions.question: What is the discrete-vs-continuous trade-off Transfusion tries to escape?
options: Discrete tokens train slower on GPUs

Discrete VQ tokens fit the transformer's vocabulary but cap image fidelity at the quantization step; continuous diffusion preserves detail but lives in a separate model with noise-schedule engineering and no clean text integration
Continuous models cannot be open-sourced
Discrete tokens require more VRAM
questions.explanation: Chameleon and Emu3 went discrete and plateaued below continuous-space diffusion quality. Transfusion's bet: keep images continuous, drop the VQ-VAE, and still train ONE model.

questions.question: How does Transfusion train one transformer on both modalities?
options: Two separate transformers share an optimizer

Two losses on one backbone: text tokens get next-token cross-entropy on the vocab head, image patches (continuous 16x16 blocks, linearly embedded like ViT input) get a diffusion loss predicting added noise — both gradients update the same weights
Images are converted to text captions first
Alternating epochs of text-only and image-only data
questions.explanation: The forward pass runs once; the loss picks one of two heads per token. <image> and </image> tags mark where the continuous patches live in the sequence.

questions.question: Why does Transfusion use causal attention for text but bidirectional attention within an image?
options: Bidirectional attention is cheaper to compute
Text needs causality or teacher forcing breaks, but an image's patches are one snapshot with no inherent order — they should all see each other, giving a block-triangular mask overall
Image patches arrive out of order from the dataloader
It was an arbitrary choice the paper never justified
questions.explanation: The mask also lets text attend to previous images and image patches attend to preceding text — causal across modality boundaries, bidirectional inside each image block.

questions.question: How does Transfusion compare to Chameleon-style discrete approaches?
options: Identical quality, identical complexity

Continuous patches + diffusion loss buy a higher image-quality ceiling (no quantization bottleneck) at the cost of two-loss training dynamics and more code complexity than single-loss NTP on discrete tokens
Transfusion is simpler in every dimension
Chameleon generates better images
questions.explanation: That's the lesson's core comparison axis: compute, quality, code complexity. Discrete = one elegant loss but tokenizer-capped fidelity; Transfusion = detail preserved but loss-balancing engineering.

questions.question: What is MMDiT's (the Stable Diffusion 3 architecture) signature design?
options: A single weight set shared identically by all modalities
Modality-specific weights at each block with joint attention at the residual stream — a close cousin of Transfusion's one-transformer-two-modality design
Three separate transformers voting
Convolutions instead of attention
questions.explanation: Text and image get their own per-block parameters but mix in joint attention. The lesson positions Transfusion and MMDiT as the same family: continuous images and text reasoning sharing one backbone.
